text: The surgical video shows the left side end-pelvic fascia being opened and dissected cordially as part of the conventional nerve sparing technique.

The Hinotori video system provides high-resolution visualization, allowing the identification of small vessels and thin tissue layers. On the right side, the endpelvic fascia is visibly preserved during the procedure.

The surgeon cuts the detrusor apron and divides the bladder and prostate. The urethral catheter is held by the third robotic arm while the posterior side is divided. The retrotrigonal layer is separated, and the bilateral vas deferens and seminal vesicles are grasped.
The surgeon performs conventional nerve sparing on the left side, using Hemlock grips to secure the lateral pedicle vessels.

On the right side, the surgeon performs nerve sparing by preserving the endoperific fascia. The anterolateral superficial vascular layer is incised to expose the prostate surface, with hemlock clips applied to each cut to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon dissects the prostate from the posterior side towards the right lateral plane and continues cordially to the apex, avoiding the use of electrocautery to prevent thermal damage to surrounding muscles and nerves.
At the apex, the surgeon strips the prostatic muscles. The deep dorsal venous complex is ligated and sutured with 3-0 Monocryl. The urethra is cut, and the prostate is removed.
In the prostate bed, the left side pelvic muscles are visibly exposed, while the right side remains covered by the end-pelvic fascia and vascular layers.
